sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 65, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 64 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 2.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I have clear positions:
- Left green: center (31, 22), boundary [[30, 21], [30, 23], [32, 23], [32, 21]]
- Right green: center (31, 40), boundary [[30, 39], [30, 41], [32, 41], [32, 39]]
- Left frame white: center (34, 13)
- Right frame white: center (28, 46)
So after UP, RIGHT:
- UP moved left green from (34, 19) to (31, 19) - wait, the left green is now at (31, 22), not (31, 19). So UP moved left green from (34, 19) to (31, 19), and then RIGHT moved left green from (31, 19) to (31, 22).
Wait, but I expected RIGHT to move the right green when the left green is at (31, 19). But it seems RIGHT moved the left green instead.
Hmm, let me reconsider. Before the UP, RIGHT actions:
- Left green was at (34, 19)
- Right green was at (31, 40)
After UP: left green moved from (34, 19) to (31, 19)
After RIGHT: left green moved from (31, 19) to (31, 22)
So RIGHT moved the left green, not the right green. This contradicts my earlier observation that when the left green is at (31, 19), RIGHT moves the right green.
Wait, maybe the control scheme is different. Let me reconsider. Earlier, I observed:
- When the left green is at (31, 19), RIGHT moves the right green.
But now, when the left green is at (31, 19), RIGHT moves the left green.
This is confusing. Maybe the control scheme changed, or maybe I misremembered the earlier observation.
Actually, looking at the left green's position, it's at (31, 22), which is to the right of (31, 19). So RIGHT did move the left green. But earlier, when the left green was at (31, 19), RIGHT moved the right green. So the control scheme must depend on something else.
Wait, maybe the control scheme depends on whether the left green is at the "control position" (31, 19). When the left green is at (31, 19), RIGHT moves the right green. But when the left green is NOT at (31, 19), RIGHT moves the left green.
But in this case, the left green WAS at (31, 19) after the UP action, and then RIGHT was pressed. So RIGHT should have moved the right green. But it moved the left green instead.
Hmm, maybe the UP action moved the left green to a different position than (31, 19). Let me check the boundary:
- Left green: boundary [[30, 21], [30, 23], [32, 23], [32, 21]]
This means the left green is at rows 30-32, cols 21-23. The center is (31, 22).
But before the UP, RIGHT actions, the left green was at (34, 19), which is rows 33-35, cols 18-20. After UP, the left green moved to rows 30-32, cols 18-20, which is (31, 19). Then RIGHT moved the left green to rows 30-32, cols 21-23, which is (31, 22).
Wait, but the left green moved from cols 18-20 to cols 21-23, which is a shift of 3 columns. So RIGHT moved the left green by 3 columns. This is consistent with earlier observations where RIGHT moves the left green by 3 columns.
So it seems like RIGHT moved the left green, not the right green. This contradicts my earlier observation. Let me reconsider the control scheme.
Actually, maybe the control scheme is:
- When the left green is at (31, 19), RIGHT moves the right green ONLY if the right green is at a certain position.
- Or maybe the control scheme is different.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider my earlier observations. Let me think about what I know for sure:
1. LEFT/RIGHT/UP/DOWN moves one of the green squares.
2. The left green square can be moved around the left frame area.
3. The right green square can be moved around the right frame area.
4. The goal is to move both green squares into their respective frames.
From the current state:
- Left green is at (31, 22), which is to the right of the left frame (34, 13).
- Right green is at (31, 40), which is to the left of the right frame (28, 46).
I need to move the left green to (34, 13) and the right green to (28, 46).
Let me try a different approach. Instead of trying to figure out the exact control scheme, let me just try moving the left green to the left frame and the right green to the right frame, one step at a time, and observe what happens.
Current state:
- Left green: (31, 22)
- Right green: (31, 40)
To move the left green to (34, 13):
- Need to move DOWN 1 (31 -> 34) and LEFT 3 (22 -> 13, but each LEFT moves by 3, so 1 LEFT).
To move the right green to (28, 46):
- Need to move UP 1 (31 -> 28) and RIGHT 2 (40 -> 46, but each RIGHT moves by 3, so 2 RIGHTs).
But I don't know how to control the right green independently. Let me try moving the left green to the left frame first, and see if that changes the control scheme.
From (31, 22), I need to move LEFT 1 (22 -> 19) and DOWN 1 (31 -> 34) and LEFT 1 (19 -> 16) and DOWN 1 (34 -> 37)... wait, this is getting complicated.
Actually, let me just try moving the left green LEFT and see what happens. If LEFT moves the left green from (31, 22) to (31, 19), then I know LEFT controls the left green. Then I can try moving the left green DOWN to (34, 19) and then LEFT to (34, 16) and then DOWN to (37, 16)... hmm, this is getting too complicated.
Let me just try a simple sequence: LEFT, DOWN, LEFT, DOWN and see where the left green ends up.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: left green at (31, 22), right green at (31, 40)
# Left frame white at (34, 13), right frame white at (28, 46)
# Try moving left green LEFT to (31, 19), then DOWN to (34, 19), then LEFT to (34, 16)
# Then check if further LEFT/DOWN moves it to (34, 13)
# Actually, let me first try LEFT to see what happens
action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
